play.title: Always Preserve the Cause Chain When Wrapping Exceptions
play.context: Preserving exception cause chains gives SREs a complete failure trail, from the original upstream error through each layer that handled it. APM can group failures by root cause, background jobs apply the correct retry strategy based on exception type, and incident responders see the full chain instead of a generic message. When cause chains are destroyed, every failure looks the same in monitoring and root-cause analysis requires manual log correlation.

rules.rule: When catching an exception and raising a new one, ensure the cause chain is preserved. Ruby automatically sets `$!.cause` when you `raise` a new exception from within a `rescue` block ; you do NOT need to pass `cause: e` explicitly.
Never use `cause: e` with `Common::Exceptions` classes; they do not accept it (it is silently ignored). Ruby's implicit cause chain handles this automatically.
If not adding context when re-raising, use bare `raise` to preserve the original exception automatically.
For custom exception classes, verify the constructor accepts `cause:` before using it. Read the class definition. If it does not accept `cause:`, rely on Ruby's implicit cause chain.

investigate_before_answering.step: Read the full method containing the rescue block to understand what exception is being caught and what new exception is being raised.
Identify the exception class being raised: does its constructor accept a `cause:` keyword argument? If not, the class itself may need to be updated before the fix can be applied.

Check if the code uses `raise "string"` pattern; this always creates a new RuntimeError and is never correct. The fix is `raise` without arguments, or a typed exception raised inside the rescue block (Ruby automatically sets the cause chain).

Determine whether the rescue block catches specific exceptions or uses bare rescue. If bare rescue, the cause chain fix should be combined with narrowing the rescue clause (see Play: Never Use Broad Rescues).

Check if Sidekiq retry behavior depends on the exception type. If so, preserving the original class is critical for correct retry strategy. Do not suggest adding `cause: e` without first verifying that the exception constructor accepts it. Read the exception class definition if needed.

severity_assessment.critical: cause chain lost in code handling PII, PHI, or benefits claims data
stringified re-raise in Sidekiq job where retry strategy depends on exception type
cause chain lost in service layer calling external APIs (BGS, MPI, Lighthouse), causing APM blackout for upstream errors
severity_assessment.high: cause chain lost when wrapping exception in controller handling user-facing requests
cause chain lost in code where HTTP status codes must propagate for correct error responses
severity_assessment.medium: cause chain lost in internal utility with no external dependencies or retry behavior
severity_assessment.false_positive: Intentional cause-chain breaks for PII scrubbing: when the original exception contains PII/PHI in its message (e.g., upstream errors that echo veteran data), raising a new exception without the cause is a deliberate security measure. Also, custom exception constructors that chain internally (e.g., `initialize` calls `super` and stores the original exception as an attribute) preserve the chain through a different mechanism than Ruby's implicit `cause`; read the exception class definition before flagging.

play.pr_comment_template:
**Always Preserve the Cause Chain When Wrapping Exceptions** | `CRITICAL`
`{{file_path}}:{{line_number}}` - {{one_line_violation_description}}
**Why this matters:** Without the cause chain, APM shows only the wrapper exception at the re-raise line. The original error's stack trace, HTTP status code, and exception class are lost. Engineers cannot determine root cause without manual log analysis. Sidekiq retry strategies may apply the wrong policy when the original exception type is destroyed.
**Suggested fix:** ```ruby rescue Faraday::ClientError raise Common::Exceptions::BadGateway.new(detail: 'Upstream client error') end # Ruby automatically preserves cause chain when raising inside rescue ```
**Verify:** - [ ] Raise happens inside a rescue block (Ruby automatically sets `$!.cause`) - [ ] No `raise "error: #{e}"` patterns remain (destroys cause chain) - [ ] No `cause: e` kwargs used with `Common::Exceptions` classes (silently ignored) - [ ] APM traces show full chain: wrapper -> original exception - [ ] Stack trace shows original failure location
[Play: Always Preserve the Cause Chain](02-preserve-cause-chains.xml)

example.name: Stringified re-raise destroys cause chain
example.bad: rescue => e raise "error: #{e}" # Creates NEW RuntimeError: original class, backtrace, and cause chain destroyed
example.good: rescue raise # Re-raises original exception with full context # Or if adding context: # raise ImportError.new("CSV import failed") # Ruby automatically sets $!.cause to the caught exception

example.name: Wraps only message string, discards exception type and backtrace
example.bad: rescue Faraday::ClientError => e raise StandardError.new(e.message) # Discards original exception class and backtrace; cause chain is set # but the wrapper is a generic StandardError with only a string message
example.good: rescue Faraday::ClientError raise Common::Exceptions::BadGateway.new(detail: 'Upstream client error') # Ruby automatically sets $!.cause to the caught Faraday exception # Typed exception maps to correct HTTP status (502)

example.name: Mobile Dependents - wraps only message string
example.bad: rescue => e raise Common::Exceptions::BackendServiceException.new( nil, detail: e.message # Only e.message preserved in error detail; exception class and structured metadata lost from response (Ruby's $!.cause chain is preserved, but consumers only see the string) )
example.good: rescue BGS::ShareError, Common::Client::Errors::ClientError raise Common::Exceptions::BadGateway.new( detail: 'BGS service unavailable' ) # Ruby automatically preserves the cause chain # BadGateway (502) correctly signals upstream failure

example.name: Re-raise typed Common::Exceptions - do NOT use cause: e
example.bad: rescue Faraday::TimeoutError => e raise Common::Exceptions::GatewayTimeout.new(detail: 'Upstream timed out', cause: e) # cause: e is silently ignored by Common::Exceptions # GatewayTimeout#errors ignores constructor arguments (it always returns i18n data); detail: and cause: are absorbed by StandardError#initialize but never used
example.good: rescue Faraday::TimeoutError raise Common::Exceptions::GatewayTimeout.new # Ruby automatically sets $!.cause to the caught Faraday::TimeoutError # GatewayTimeout takes no arguments; it always returns 504
